content.problemStatement:
In a video game country, there are NNN cities connected by N−1N-1N−1 bi-directional roads, forming a tree network. The ithi_{th}ith​ city has an integer associated with it, called difficulty DiD_iDi​.

A game map is represented by a pair of integers (X,Y)(X,Y)(X,Y), such that (1≤X≤N)(1\leq X\leq N)(1≤X≤N) and (0≤Y≤N−1)(0 \leq Y \leq {N-1})(0≤Y≤N−1). All nodes within a distance YYY from the node XXX are included in this game map. (Note that the distance between two nodes is the number of edges on the shortest path between them). The difficulty of a game map is the sum of difficulties of all nodes in the map. When you start the game, the system chooses a game map as the map for your current match, uniformly at random out of all N2N^2N2 possible game maps.

To maximize your chances of winning, you want to re-order the difficulties (that is, permute the array DDD) such that the expected value of the difficulty of a randomly chosen match is minimized. What is the minimum expectation on the difficulty of a match, if you re-order the array D optimally?

The answer can be expressed as a fraction XY\frac{X}{Y}YX​, where XXX and YYY are coprime integers. You should print X⋅Y−1X\cdot Y^{-1}X⋅Y−1 modulo 109+710^9+7109+7, where Y−1Y^{-1}Y−1 is the multiplicative inverse of YYY modulo 109+710^9+7109+7.


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of multiple lines of input.

The first line of each test case contains a single integer NNN — the number of cities in the video game country.
The next line contains NNN space separated integers, representing the array DDD.
The next N−1N-1N−1 lines describe the edges. The iii-th of these N−1N-1N−1 lines contains two space-separated integers uiu_iui​ and viv_ivi​, denoting an undirected edge between uiu_iui​ and viv_ivi​.
It is guaranteed that the given nodes and edges form a tree.




Output Format

For each test case, print the answer modulo 109+710^9+7109+7
constraints: 1 ≤ N ≤ 3·10⁵ (sum of N over all test cases ≤ 3·10⁵)
The given edges form a tree.
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. For each test case: first line contains integer N (number of cities). Second line contains N integers D1 … DN (the difficulties). The next N‑1 lines each contain two integers u and v denoting an undirected edge of the tree.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line containing the minimum possible expected difficulty of a randomly chosen game map, expressed as X·Y⁻¹ modulo 1 000 000 007 (where Y⁻¹ is the modular inverse of Y).

solution.approach: For a fixed permutation of difficulties, the expected difficulty equals (1/N²)·Σv Dv·cntv where cntv is the number of pairs (X,Y) such that the node v lies within distance Y from X. For a given centre X, Y can be any value ≥ dist(X,v), therefore the number of suitable Y is N‑dist(X,v). Hence cntv = ΣX (N‑dist(X,v)) = N²‑Sv, where Sv = ΣX dist(X,v) is the sum of distances from v to all nodes. The tree allows us to compute Sv for every node in O(N) using a classic two‑pass DP (post‑order to obtain subtree sizes and partial distance sums, then a rerooting pass). Once all cntv are known we want to minimise Σ Dv·cntv. By the rearrangement inequality the minimum is achieved by sorting the difficulties in non‑decreasing order and the cnt values in non‑increasing order and pairing them. The answer is ( Σ sortedD[i]·sortedCnt[i] ) / N². All operations are performed modulo MOD = 1 000 000 007, using modular inverse of N².
solution.timeComplexity: O(N log N) per test case because of the two sorts; the DP passes are O(N).
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N) for adjacency list, subtree sizes, distance sums and auxiliary arrays.
